summary: Stellar Estimated Prophet > Eyes jam, tight Samson/Hey Pocky Way flow
blurb: The July‑22 1990 show delivers a monster Estimated Prophet → Eyes of the World stretch and a seamless Samson & Delilah into Hey Pocky Way transition. Brent’s vocals on Far From Me and Knockin’ On Heaven’s Door are highlights, while Jerry’s soaring solos drive the set.
review: On July 22, 1990 at the World Music Theater in Tinley Park, the Dead turned in a high‑energy performance that still shines decades later. The first set opens with the rare “Box of Rain” and rolls through tight renditions of “West L.A. Fadeaway,” “Loser,” and an emotive “Far From Me.” The second set is where the night truly rockets: a monster “Estimated Prophet → Eyes of the World” jam, flanked by a flawless “Samson & Delilah → Hey Pocky Way” segue, and a soaring “The Wheel” that leads into a soulful “Gimme Some Lovin’ → Stella Blue → Throwing Stones → Turn On Your Lovelight.” The night closes on a high note with an inspired “Knockin' On Heaven's Door.” Across multiple soundboard sources the band is praised for tight rhythm, Brent’s commanding organ and vocal work, and Jerry’s blistering leads, despite a few mixed‑quality recordings that note occasional vocal pickup issues. Overall, the show is a must‑listen snapshot of the Dead in prime form during their 1990 summer tour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across four out of six recordings: multiple reviewers call the show 'exceptional', highlight the Estimated Prophet → Eyes jam, Samson/Hey Pocky Way flow and Brent's vocal performances. No consistent negative criticism appears; mixed reviews only note minor acoustic issues. This consensus warrants a high positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Remastered soundboard with crisp, balanced mix; captures the full energy of the performance without dropouts.

key_highlights: Monster Estimated Prophet → Eyes of the World jam that drives the second set.
Seamless Samson & Delilah into Hey Pocky Way transition, showcasing tight group interplay.
Brent Mydland's standout vocals on Far From Me and Knockin' On Heaven's Door.
Exceptional recording quality on the recommended SBD remaster.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers soaring solos on West L.A. Fadeaway, The Wheel and Knockin' On Heaven's Door; overall leads are tight and fiery, with only occasional slips noted in mixed‑quality tapes.
band_performance.Phil: Provides a rock‑solid bass foundation—especially on Samson & Delilah and Gimme Some Lovin' intro—and adds emotive depth to Stella Blue.
band_performance.Bob: Strong vocal delivery throughout; minor lyric blips on Hell In A Bucket and brief harmony roughness on Stella Blue are the only noticeable slips.
band_performance.Brent: Top‑notch organ work and commanding vocals, highlighted on Far From Me, Hey Pocky Way, and Knockin' On Heaven's Door; serves as the emotional centerpiece of the night.
band_performance.Mickey: Awesome post‑drums with punchy fills; contributes to the spacey vibe during jams and keeps tight support throughout.
band_performance.Billy: Solid rhythmic backbone alongside Mickey; generally in sync, though a few reviewers note slight timing drift later in the set.
